title: Compression Enables Generalization: Wake-Sleep Cycles for Logic Programming with LLM Integration
description: Knowledge bases in logic programming grow through fact accumulation but do not learn. We present DreamLog, a system that compresses its knowledge base through wake-sleep cycles, discovering rules that generalize to unseen entities, together with a test protocol that makes the compression-versus-memorization distinction auditable by pairing fact bases over invented vocabulary with a raw-LLM baseline on identical inputs. On a synthetic crafting domain with invented terms unknown to the LLM, symbolic compression alone recovers 53% of held-out generalizations and the full pipeline 63%, while a raw LLM recovers 0%; recursive transitive closures are recovered at 100% even over invented vocabulary. On a canonical family tree the full pipeline reaches 80% recall at 100% precision. Refining the acceptance criterion from clause count to a rename-invariant, parameter-free bits-based description length makes it structure-sensitive, and across twenty-two synthetic domains the degree of compression predicts held-out recovery (Pearson r=0.52, n=22, p=0.014). Compared against three inductive-logic-programming paradigms (Popper, Metagol, ILASP), DreamLog sits at the low-supervision end of a prior-knowledge spectrum, recovering cross-predicate and recursive rules from an unannotated fact base that the baselines reach only with labeled examples, a mode bias, or metarule templates. These results are consistent with the Solomonoff induction thesis: shorter programs generalize better.
notes: Code and experiment registry available at https://github.com/queelius/dreamlog. All experiment scripts (EX01-EX41) with full provenance and reproducible results; per-run metadata envelopes record git commit, environment, package versions, seeds, and output hashes.
